perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed in acc ordance with the laws of England and Wales without giving effect to conflict of law principles, and the Parties agree to submit to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the English courts.
perturbation.changed_text: Any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement shall constitute a breach that may cause irreparable inj ury, not readily measurable in money, and for which the Providing Party , without waiving any other rights or remedies at law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief or other equitable relief. Any such right or remedy and any and all other rights or remedies provided for herein shall be cumulative and not exclusive and in addition to any and all other rights or remedies which the Providing Party may have under this Agreement or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law clause is relocated to the remedies section. This obscures the governing law of the agreement, placing it within a section describing the remedies for a breach of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original location of the governing law clause ensures clarity and predictability regarding which jurisdiction's laws will govern the agreement. Moving it to the remedies section obfuscates this and undermines the principle that governing law should be clearly and conspicuously stated, potentially creating ambiguity as to which laws apply in different aspects of the agreement. Rome I Regulation dictates law governing contractual obligations in civil and commercial matters and aims to provide legal certainty in cross-border transactions.

perturbation.justification: The structural flaw of relocating the governing law clause to the remedies section creates a legal contradiction, as it obscures the clarity and enforceability of the agreement's legal foundation. The law explanation states the change 'undermines the principle that governing law should be clearly and conspicuously stated, potentially creating ambiguity as to which laws apply in different aspects of the agreement.' While the provided law snippets are not helpful, the placement of the governing law within a remedies section makes it difficult for parties to easily locate and interpret which laws govern the entire agreement. This violates the standard practice of having a clear governing law clause, as indicated by the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Information will be used solely for giving effect to the Business Under Discussion and further that it will not disclose to any person (i) the Information and (ii) the fact that the Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or that the Receiving Party have inspected any portion of the Information, for an y reason or purpose whatsoever, unless the Providing Party has consented in writing to such disclosure or except as stated otherwise herein or as required by law, order, decree, regulation, or governmental agency request. The term “person” as used in these provisions shall be broadly interpreted to include without limitation any corporation, company, partnership, individual or group.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of such communication the Receiving Party shall destroy promptly all documents furnished and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in who le or in part of such Information. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party shall be entitled to retain that portion of the Information for legal, regulatory or internal compliance purposes, which will continue to be treated as confidential on t he terms hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the document destruction clause to replace the confidentiality clause. This buries the obligation of confidentiality in a section about termination and document destruction. This might lead a reasonable person to miss the scope of their non-disclosure obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: European Union Directive 2016/943 requires that measures are taken to protect trade secrets. By hiding the confidentiality obligations within the document destruction section, it undermines the effort to take reasonable steps to keep the information secret, making it more difficult to pursue legal action for trade secret misappropriation under the directive. The directive highlights the importance of actively keeping information confidential, which is undermined by this move.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the intention of Directive (EU) 2016/943. The original contract language clearly established a confidentiality obligation. By moving this obligation to a section on document destruction, the changed text weakens the enforceability and clarity of the confidentiality clause. As stated in the Law Explanation, this undermines reasonable steps to keep information secret, potentially hindering legal action for misappropriation. The 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition states that contradictions arise when legal disclosures are placed in misleading sections, making them difficult to interpret. Moving the clause meets this definition, thus creating a contradiction. While the snippet doesn't explicitly state where confidentiality clauses must be placed, the implicit requirement is that they be clear and easily accessible, which is not the case in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: Any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement shall constitute a breach that may cause irreparable inj ury, not readily measurable in money, and for which the Providing Party , without waiving any other rights or remedies at law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief or other equitable relief. Any such right or remedy and any and all other rights or remedies provided for herein shall be cumulative and not exclusive and in addition to any and all other rights or remedies which the Providing Party may have under this Agreement or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of loss or dama ge to the Providing Party as a result of breach of the provisions of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall be liable only in respect of loss or damage resulting directly from the breach, and no liability will be accepted in respect of any special, indi rect, incidental or consequential losses or damages whatsoever (including, but not limited to, lost profits or revenue) suffered by the Providing Party or any person connected with the Providing Party, arising out of the performance or breach of this Agree ment, whether claims for said losses or damages are premised on contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: By relocating the clause on injunctive relief from the remedies section to the damages section, the ability of the Providing Party to seek immediate court intervention is obscured. It suggests that only monetary damages can be recovered.
perturbation.law_citation: UK Sale of Goods Act 1979, s 52
perturbation.law_explanation: The UK Sale of Goods Act 1979 (s 52) and related principles of contract law allow for specific performance or injunctive relief in certain circumstances. By obfuscating the clause about injunctive relief, the contract suggests the Providing Party might only be able to seek damages, potentially barring them from seeking an injunction to stop a breach from continuing or occurring in the first place, contrary to the availability of this remedy under UK law.

perturbation.justification: The modification appears to contradict the principle of injunctive relief available under UK contract law. While Snippet 1 is a general reference to the Sale of Goods Act, the Law Explanation specifically mentions section 52 and related contract law principles allowing for specific performance or injunctive relief. The original text explicitly states the Providing Party's right to seek injunctive relief. The changed text obscures this right by focusing solely on monetary damages and limiting liability, potentially preventing the Providing Party from obtaining an injunction, which conflicts with the law's provision for such remedies.

perturbation.original_text: In th e event that the Receiving Party is required by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Receiving Party agrees that it will provide the Providing Party with prompt written notice of such request(s) prior to the required disclosure and the Providing Party shall use reasonable efforts, at its own expense, to seek a protective o rder or other appropriate remedy. If a protective order or other remedy is not obtained and a waiver hereunder is not received, the Receiving Party shall disclose only that p ortion of the Information which is legally required and shall use its best effort s to obtain assurances that confidential treatment will be accorded to such Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of loss or dama ge to the Providing Party as a result of breach of the provisions of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall be liable only in respect of loss or damage resulting directly from the breach, and no liability will be accepted in respect of any special, indi rect, incidental or consequential losses or damages whatsoever (including, but not limited to, lost profits or revenue) suffered by the Providing Party or any person connected with the Providing Party, arising out of the performance or breach of this Agree ment, whether claims for said losses or damages are premised on contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the legally compelled disclosure clause with a limitation of liability clause. The clause specifying required disclosure is crucial because it outlines the procedure for when the Receiving Party has to disclose confidential information because of legal or governmental pressure. By replacing that with limitations of damages, it hides this clause which is not appropriate as it has major legal implications. Such changes could affect the transparency and comprehension of legally mandated procedures.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Civil Procedure Rules (CPR) in the UK mandate disclosure obligations in legal proceedings. Removing the section that previously clarified what to do if disclosure is required by law, order, decree, regulation, or governmental agency request with a limitation of damages is contradictory. It does not acknowledge these obligations and it may mislead parties by downplaying these crucial legal mandates.

perturbation.justification: The original text contains a clause that describes how the Receiving Party should act if they are legally compelled to disclose confidential information. The changed text replaces this clause with a limitation of liability clause. The Perturbation Explanation notes that the disclosure clause outlines the procedure for when the Receiving Party has to disclose confidential information because of legal or governmental pressure, and replacing that with limitations of damages hides this clause which is not appropriate as it has major legal implications. The Law Explanation states that removing the section that previously clarified what to do if disclosure is required by law, order, decree, regulation, or governmental agency request with a limitation of damages is contradictory. Because the original text contains a legally required clause and the changed text removes that clause, it is a structural flaw that creates a legal contradiction.
